did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning (specifically EfficientNet models) and a proposed data augmentation strategy (Low-Cost-Augment) to perform skin lesion classification on dermatology datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2017, Derm7pt). It also utilizes Grad-CAM++ for model interpretability in the context of skin cancer screening.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work, including the proposal of a new data augmentation strategy (Low-Cost-Augment), the use of EfficientNet models, and the validation of results on specific datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2017, Derm7pt) with reported metrics such as BACC and AUC.

title: A Low-Cost High-Performance Data Augmentation for Deep Learning-Based Skin Lesion Classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the need for low-cost, high-performance data augmentation strategies for intelligent skin cancer screening in resource-limited settings. The authors propose a novel data augmentation strategy called Low-Cost-Augment (LCA) with a search space of 101, which is combined with EfficientNet models for skin lesion classification. The approach was evaluated on three dermatology datasets: HAM10000, ISIC 2017, and Derm7pt, using metrics such as Balanced Accuracy (BACC) and Area Under the Curve (AUC). The method achieved a best BACC of 0.853 on HAM10000, an average AUC of 0.909 on ISIC 2017, and a BACC of 0.735 on Derm7pt, outperforming many existing models without external data. Additionally, Grad-CAM++ heatmaps were used to verify the model's focus on relevant lesion features. The results demonstrate that the proposed strategy significantly reduces computational cost while maintaining high diagnostic performance, facilitating deployment on portable AI devices.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on 'skin lesion classification' using EfficientNet models. It reports Balanced Accuracy (BACC), Accuracy, Specificity, Precision, and AUC. Specific tasks include multiclass classification on HAM10000 (7 classes) and Derm7pt, and binary classification on ISIC 2017 (melanoma vs. others; seborrheic keratosis vs. others).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, publicly accessible for the 2018 skin lesion analysis challenge organized by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). Contains 10,015 images with pathologically verified labels for 7 classes.

use.training: Used for training and validation via 5-fold cross-validation during the augmentation search phase. Also used for fine-tuning EfficientNet models.
use.validation: Used for validation during the augmentation search phase (via 5-fold cross-validation).
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the proposed data augmentation strategy and EfficientNet models (BACC reported).
use.other: Pretraining source for EfficientNet models before fine-tuning on other datasets.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin dermoscopy image dataset consisting of images screened for privacy and quality assurance, with gold standard diagnoses. Used for binary classification tasks.

use.training: Used for training EfficientNet models fine-tuned from HAM10000.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance (AUC reported).
use.other: Evaluated for binary classification tasks: melanoma vs. others, and seborrheic keratosis vs. others.

datasets_used.name: Derm7pt
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 1011 images for each image modality (total 2022 images), including patient metadata and the 7-point checklist. Contains diagnoses for BCC, NV, MEL, MISC, and SK.
classes_used: Nevus (NV) (subtypes: blue, clark, combined, congenital, dermal, recurrent, reed)
Miscellaneous (MISC) (subtypes: dermatofibroma, lentigo, melanosis, miscellaneous, vascular lesion)

use.training: Used for fine-tuning HAM10000-pretrained EfficientNet models (413 images used for training in the described experiment).
use.testing: Used for testing model performance (BACC reported). 395 test images were used to verify performance in the described experiment.
use.other: Evaluated for generalization of the searched augmentation method.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to split the HAM10000 training set into training and validation subsets for augmentation search and model evaluation, ensuring no data leakage between folds.
Used to report the variability of performance metrics (AUC on ISIC 2017 and BACC on Derm7pt) across multiple runs or folds.
Used to generate final predictions by averaging predictions from 16 equidistantly cropped regions of interest (ROIs) from each image, improving prediction performance.
Used to handle class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset by adding enhanced weights to underrepresented classes (DF and VASC) during training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made based on reported metrics (BACC, AUC) and standard deviations across cross-validation folds or runs, without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The best BACC of 0.853 was achieved by EfficientNet b2 using the proposed Low-Cost-Augment (LCA) strategy at probability 0.3. This ranks first in the 'single-model and no-external-database' channel for the ISIC 2018 Lesion Diagnosis Challenge (Task 3). The BACC values for EfficientNets b0-b7 showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing, with deviations fluctuating within ±0.033 in 5-fold cross-validation. The model achieved a diagnostic specificity greater than 0.983 for all classes and diagnostic accuracy greater than 0.91 for all classes except NV. Sensitivity for melanoma was superior to ensembling models and those using external datasets.

ISIC 2017.notes: EfficientNet b1 achieved the best Average AUC of 0.909 (±0.015), exceeding most ensembling models and those using external datasets. The study focused on EfficientNet b0-b3 because b4-b7 showed signs of overfitting on this dataset. Sensitivity for Melanoma (MEL) was ahead of all others, especially Díaz [32], and sensitivity for Seborrheic Keratosis (SK) was second only to Matsunaga et al. [31]. Table 3 reports specific metrics for 'Our approach (EfficientNet b1)': Avg. AUC 0.909 ± 0.015, BACC 0.821 ± 0.025, Sensitivity (MEL) 0.743 ± 0.016, Sensitivity (SK) 0.830 ± 0.022, Specificity (MEL) 0.922 ± 0.019, Specificity (SK) 0.856 ± 0.019.

Derm7pt.notes: EfficientNet b0 achieved the best BACC of 0.735 (±0.018), ahead of all other related studies. Table 3 reports specific metrics for 'Our approach (EfficientNet b0)': BACC 0.735 ± 0.018 (mean), Avg. AUC 0.913 ± 0.021 (mean), Sensitivity (MEL) 0.92 ± 0.020 (mean), Specificity (MEL) 0.735 ± 0.018 (mean). The study focused on EfficientNet b0-b3 due to overfitting phenomena observed in b4-b7 on datasets of ISIC-skin 2017 scale.

architecture_used.main_model: EfficientNet (specifically EfficientNet b0, b1, b2, and b3 were evaluated; b2 was best for HAM10000, b1 for ISIC 2017, and b0 for Derm7pt)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained on ImageNet, then fine-tuned on HAM10000, ISIC 2017, or Derm7pt
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 (randomly cropped from original images of size 600x450 or unscaled images)

architecture_details.note: b4-b7 showed overfitting on ISIC 2017 and Derm7pt datasets, so analysis focused on b0-b3 for those datasets

training_details.loss_function: Multiclass weighted cross-entropy loss (standard cross-entropy with enhanced weights for underrepresented classes DF and VASC)
training_details.learning_rate: Starting lr = 0.001, reduced by factor of 1/10 after 20 epochs, then every 10 epochs
training_details.batch_size: Model-specific values of 2^n (refer to Table S3 in supplementary materials)
training_details.evaluation_strategy: Multicrop evaluation: 16 equidistantly cropped 224x224 ROIs from each image, predictions averaged

classification_or_output_head.output_dimension: Modified to match number of classes (7 for HAM10000, 5 for Derm7pt, binary for ISIC 2017 tasks)

classes.ISIC_2017: Binary (Melanoma vs Others; Seborrheic Keratosis vs Others)

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing/cropping to 224x224 pixels (randomly cropped from original images of size 600x450 or unscaled images)
Multicrop evaluation strategy: 16 equidistantly cropped 224x224 regions of interest (ROIs) from each image for prediction aggregation
Data augmentation strategies including flip, scale changes, Gaussian noise, and color tone shift (part of the Low-Cost-Augment search space)

Image normalization (implied by standard deep learning practices and cross-entropy loss formulation, though specific normalization parameters like mean/std are not explicitly detailed in the provided text)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: A proposed search space of 101 subpolicies combining geometric and color transforms. It includes flip, scale changes, Gaussian noise, and color tone shift. The search space consists of 12 subpolicies for color strategies and 6 geometric operations (each geometric operation selected twice to ensure equal probability). One subpolicy is randomly executed for each image during training. The optimal execution probability was found to be 0.3 for the HAM10000 dataset.
Included as a geometric transform within the LCA search space.
Included as a geometric transform within the LCA search space.
Included as a transform within the LCA search space.
Included as a transform within the LCA search space, part of the 12 color strategies.
Used during inference/testing. 16 equidistantly cropped 224x224 regions of interest (ROIs) from each image were used, and predictions were averaged.
Images were equally cropped to 224x224 pixels randomly before being plugged into the DCNNs.

conclusions: The proposed Low-Cost-Augment (LCA) data augmentation strategy significantly reduces computational costs compared to existing methods like AutoAugment and Fast AutoAugment, making it suitable for low-resource settings.

The LCA strategy improves skin lesion classification performance, achieving a best Balanced Accuracy (BACC) of 0.853 on the HAM10000 dataset, which ranks first among single-model approaches without external databases in the ISIC 2018 Lesion Diagnosis Challenge.

The method demonstrates strong generalization capabilities, achieving an average AUC of 0.909 (±0.015) on the ISIC 2017 dataset and a BACC of 0.735 (±0.018) on the Derm7pt dataset, outperforming many ensembling models and those using external datasets.

EfficientNet b2 was identified as the optimal model for the HAM10000 dataset, while EfficientNet b1 and b0 performed best for ISIC 2017 and Derm7pt, respectively; larger models (b4-b7) showed signs of overfitting on smaller datasets.
Grad-CAM++ heatmaps verified that the model accurately focuses on relevant lesion features, supporting the scientific rationality of the model-based diagnosis.
The data augmentation strategy obtained from one dataset (HAM10000) cannot be effectively transferred to other datasets, indicating the need for dataset-specific augmentation search.
The approach facilitates the deployment of low-cost, portable, and AI-based mobile devices for skin cancer screening and therapeutic guidance in rural or underdeveloped communities.
important_remarks: The proposed Low-Cost-Augment (LCA) strategy is dataset-specific and cannot be effectively transferred to other datasets, necessitating dataset-specific augmentation search.
Larger EfficientNet models (b4-b7) exhibited signs of overfitting on the ISIC 2017 and Derm7pt datasets, limiting the analysis to smaller variants (b0-b3) for those datasets.
The study did not perform formal hypothesis testing or report p-values; performance comparisons were based on reported metrics and standard deviations without statistical significance testing.

The method's Balanced Accuracy (BACC) on HAM10000 (0.853) remains behind the top-performing models on the ISIC 2018 challenge leaderboards by a margin of 0.032, potentially due to insufficient data augmentation representation or overfitting of a single DCNN.
The HAM10000 dataset contains a significant class imbalance, with only 115 cases of Dermatofibroma (DF) and 142 cases of Vascular lesions (VASC), which were addressed using multiclass weighted loss.
The ISIC 2017 dataset has a very small number of Actinic Keratosis/Bowen's diseases (AKIEC) cases (only 2), limiting the generalizability of findings for this specific lesion type.
The Derm7pt dataset used for testing is relatively small (1011 images per modality) and includes miscellaneous diagnoses, which may affect the robustness of the classification performance.

The study relies on publicly accessible datasets which may have uneven distribution of disease types and relatively small data sizes compared to large-scale clinical datasets, potentially limiting real-world clinical replication.
Grad-CAM++ heatmaps were used for interpretability but their ability to locate multiple lesions in complex scenes has not yet been verified.
